questions.q: For the $7$-adic absolute value, which statement is correct?
questions.explain: $v_7(7)=1$, so $|7|_7 = 7^{-1} = 1/7$. Divisibility by $p$ makes a number $p$-adically small.

questions.q: Which inequality is the defining ultrametric property of $|\cdot|_p$?
choices: $|x+y|_p \le \max(|x|_p,|y|_p)$
$|x+y|_p \ge \max(|x|_p,|y|_p)$
questions.explain: Non-Archimedean absolute values satisfy the strong triangle inequality $|x+y|_p\le\max(|x|_p,|y|_p)$, which is strictly stronger than the usual triangle inequality.

hard.q: Compute $|2^{10} \cdot 3^5 / 5^3|_2$. Express your answer as a fraction; enter the decimal value.
Ostrowski's theorem classifies all non-trivial absolute values on $\mathbb{Q}$. Which statement is correct?
choices: There are infinitely many inequivalent non-Archimedean absolute values and exactly one Archimedean one (up to equivalence)
Every absolute value on $\mathbb{Q}$ is equivalent to either the usual absolute value $|\cdot|_\infty$ or a $p$-adic absolute value $|\cdot|_p$ for some prime $p$
The only absolute values on $\mathbb{Q}$ are the $p$-adic ones; the real absolute value is not an absolute value
There exist absolute values on $\mathbb{Q}$ not equivalent to any $|\cdot|_p$ or $|\cdot|_\infty$
hard.explain: Ostrowski's theorem: every non-trivial absolute value on $\mathbb{Q}$ is equivalent to $|\cdot|_\infty$ (Archimedean) or to $|\cdot|_p$ for a unique prime $p$ (non-Archimedean). These exhaust all possibilities.

hard.q: The $p$-adic completion $\mathbb{Q}_p$ of $\mathbb{Q}$ is constructed via Cauchy sequences. Which of the following is the correct ordering of the construction steps?
choices: Define $|\cdot|_p$ on $\mathbb{Q}$ → form all Cauchy sequences → quotient by null sequences → verify field axioms
Form all Cauchy sequences → define $|\cdot|_p$ on them → quotient by sequences converging to $1$ → extend multiplication
Quotient $\mathbb{Z}$ by $(p^n)$ for each $n$ → take inverse limit $\varprojlim \mathbb{Z}/p^n\mathbb{Z}$ → this gives $\mathbb{Q}_p$ directly
Both (a) and (c) are valid constructions yielding isomorphic completions
hard.explain: Step (a) is the standard analytic completion: define the norm, take Cauchy sequences, quotient by null sequences, check the result is a field. Step (c) is the profinite / inverse-limit construction, which yields $\mathbb{Z}_p$; inverting $p$ then gives $\mathbb{Q}_p$. Both routes produce canonically isomorphic objects.

questions.q: In an ultrametric space, which statement is true about any two closed balls $B_1, B_2$?
choices: They always intersect
They are either disjoint or one contains the other
They always have equal radius
Their union is always a ball
questions.explain: Because every point inside a ball is its center, two balls with a common point must be the same ball of radius $\max(r_1,r_2)$ — so any two balls are either disjoint or nested.

choices: $8$, by the triangle inequality
$5$, by the ultrametric equality when norms differ
$\max(3,5)=5$ only as an upper bound; could be anything in $[0,5]$
Cannot be determined without more data
questions.explain: When $|x|_p \ne |y|_p$, the ultrametric inequality becomes an equality: $|x+y|_p = \max(|x|_p,|y|_p)$. This is the 'isoceles triangle' principle.

questions.q: How many connected components does $\mathbb{Q}_p$ have as a topological space? (Answer with the cardinality of singletons per point, i.e. $1$ per point means totally disconnected — here give the size of the connected component of any fixed point.)
questions.explain: $\mathbb{Q}_p$ is totally disconnected: the only connected subsets are singletons. So each point is its own connected component, of size $1$.

hard.q: In an ultrametric space every open ball is also closed. Which chain of reasoning correctly establishes this?
choices: The complement of $B(a,r)=\{x:|x-a|_p < r\}$ is open: for any $y\notin B(a,r)$ the ball $B(y,|y-a|_p)$ is disjoint from $B(a,r)$ by the nesting property, so the complement is a union of open balls
Open balls are closed because $\mathbb{Q}_p$ is compact
The boundary of every ball is empty since there are no points with $|x-a|_p = r$ for most values of $r$, so open and closed balls coincide trivially for all $r$
Both (a) and (c) together give the correct argument
hard.explain: Both parts contribute. (a): the nesting/disjointness of ultrametric balls makes the complement a union of open balls, hence open — so $B(a,r)$ is closed. (c): the $p$-adic norm takes values in $\{p^n:n\in\mathbb{Z}\}\cup\{0\}$, so for $r$ not a power of $p$ the 'sphere' $\{|x-a|_p=r\}$ is empty and the open and closed balls of that radius coincide trivially. Both observations together explain clopen-ness for all radii.

hard.q: Which of the following is a counterexample showing that the usual real-analysis fact 'a Cauchy sequence converges iff it is bounded' does NOT characterize $\mathbb{Q}_p$-Cauchy sequences in a new way?
choices: In $\mathbb{Q}_p$ the sequence $a_n = p^n$ is bounded ($|a_n|_p = p^{-n}\to 0$) and Cauchy, converging to $0$
In $\mathbb{Q}_p$ every bounded sequence is Cauchy, unlike in $\mathbb{R}$
The sequence $a_n = n$ is Cauchy in $\mathbb{Q}_p$ but unbounded in the usual sense, showing $p$-adic and real boundedness diverge
No counterexample exists; bounded iff Cauchy holds in $\mathbb{Q}_p$ too
hard.explain: $a_n=p^n$ satisfies $|a_n|_p=p^{-n}\to 0$, so it is $p$-adically small (hence Cauchy with limit $0$), even though $p^n\to\infty$ in the real sense. This illustrates how $p$-adic 'size' inverts the real intuition of large powers of $p$ being large numbers.

hard.q: In $\mathbb{Z}_5$, the open ball $B(0,1)=\{x\in\mathbb{Z}_5:|x|_5<1\}$ equals $5\mathbb{Z}_5$. As an additive group, $\mathbb{Z}_5/5\mathbb{Z}_5\cong\mathbb{Z}/5\mathbb{Z}$. How many cosets does $5\mathbb{Z}_5$ have in $\mathbb{Z}_5$?
hard.explain: $\mathbb{Z}_5/5\mathbb{Z}_5\cong\mathbb{Z}/5\mathbb{Z}$ has order $5$, so there are exactly $5$ cosets. These correspond to the $5$ possible first digits $\{0,1,2,3,4\}$ of a $5$-adic integer.

choices: $-1 = -1$, terminating
$-1 = 4 + 4\cdot 5 + 4\cdot 5^2 + \cdots$
$-1$ does not lie in $\mathbb{Z}_5$
questions.explain: Using the geometric series identity $(p-1)\sum_{i\ge 0} p^i = -1$ in $|\cdot|_p$: with $p=5$, $4(1+5+5^2+\cdots) = -1$ in $\mathbb{Z}_5$.

questions.q: What is the first digit $a_0$ in the $3$-adic expansion of $7$ (i.e. $7 \bmod 3$)?
Two elements $x,y \in \mathbb{Z}_p$ satisfy $|x-y|_p = p^{-n}$ precisely when:
choices: their first $n$ digits agree and the $n$th disagrees
$x$ and $y$ are congruent mod $p^n$ but differ mod $p^{n+1}$
both (a) and (b)
they agree only on $a_0$
questions.explain: Digits agreeing through index $n-1$ is exactly the condition $x\equiv y\pmod{p^n}$, and disagreeing at index $n$ means $x\not\equiv y\pmod{p^{n+1}}$. Both descriptions are equivalent.

hard.q: Find the $5$-adic expansion digit $a_1$ of $-7$ in $\mathbb{Z}_5$, i.e. the coefficient of $5^1$ in the expansion $-7 = a_0 + a_1\cdot 5 + a_2\cdot 5^2 + \cdots$ with each $a_i\in\{0,1,2,3,4\}$.
A rational number $r\in\mathbb{Q}$ has an eventually periodic $p$-adic expansion. Which statement correctly characterises when the expansion is purely periodic (periodic from digit $a_0$)?
choices: Exactly when $r\in\mathbb{Z}_{(p)}$ (the localisation of $\mathbb{Z}$ at $p$), i.e. the denominator is not divisible by $p$
Exactly when $r\in\mathbb{Z}$
Exactly when $r$ is a positive integer
Every rational has a purely periodic $p$-adic expansion
hard.explain: The $p$-adic expansion of $r$ is purely periodic iff $r\in\mathbb{Z}_p$ (i.e. $|r|_p\le 1$), which for $r\in\mathbb{Q}$ means $v_p(r)\ge 0$, i.e. the denominator of $r$ (in lowest terms) is coprime to $p$. Negative $p$-adic valuation introduces a finite 'principal part' of negative-index terms, breaking pure periodicity.

hard.q: Which of the following correctly orders the steps to write the $p$-adic expansion of a negative integer $-N$ ($N>0$)?
choices: Compute $-N\bmod p$ to get $a_0$ → subtract $a_0$, divide by $p$ to get $-N_1$ → repeat; the expansion terminates after $\lceil\log_p N\rceil$ steps
Compute $a_0=(-N)\bmod p\in\{0,\ldots,p-1\}$ → set $N_1 = (-N-a_0)/p$ (still a negative integer or $0$) → repeat to get all digits; the process never terminates, yielding an infinite expansion
Write $-N = p^k\cdot u$ with $\gcd(u,p)=1$ → expand $u$ → prepend $k$ zero digits
The expansion of $-N$ is obtained by negating each digit of $N$
hard.explain: For a negative integer $-N$, $(-N)\bmod p\in\{1,\ldots,p-1\}$ (nonzero since $p\nmid N$ generically), and $(-N-a_0)/p$ is again a negative integer; this repeats forever. The $p$-adic expansion of a negative integer is an infinite non-terminating sequence, unlike the finite expansion of a positive integer.

questions.q: Hensel's lemma (simple form) lifts $x_0$ with $f(x_0)\equiv 0\pmod p$ to a root in $\mathbb{Z}_p$ provided:
choices: $f$ has integer coefficients
$f'(x_0) \not\equiv 0 \pmod p$
$f'(x_0) \equiv 0 \pmod p$
$f(x_0) \equiv 0 \pmod{p^2}$
questions.explain: The derivative being a unit mod $p$ (i.e. nonzero mod $p$) is what makes Newton's iteration $x_{n+1}=x_n - f(x_n)/f'(x_n)$ well-defined and convergent in $\mathbb{Z}_p$.

questions.q: Seeding Hensel for $f(x) = x^2 - 2$ over $\mathbb{Z}_7$ with $x_0 = 3$, the next iterate $x_1 = x_0 + a_1\cdot 7$ uses digit $a_1 = -(x_0^2-2)/7 \cdot (2x_0)^{-1} \bmod 7$. Compute $a_1$.
questions.explain: $(3^2-2)/7 = 1$. Also $2\cdot 3 = 6$, and $6^{-1} \equiv 6 \pmod 7$. So $a_1 \equiv -1\cdot 6 \equiv -6 \equiv 1 \pmod 7$. Hence $x_1 = 3 + 1\cdot 7 = 10$, and indeed $10^2 = 100 \equiv 2 \pmod{49}$.

choices: No, because $-1 < 0$
Yes, because $2^2 = 4 \equiv -1 \pmod 5$ and $2\cdot 2 = 4 \not\equiv 0 \pmod 5$, so Hensel lifts
Only in $\mathbb{Q}_3$
Only in $\mathbb{Q}_2$
questions.explain: Take $f(x)=x^2+1$. Then $f(2)=5\equiv 0\pmod 5$ and $f'(2)=4\not\equiv 0\pmod 5$. Hensel produces a unique $i\in\mathbb{Z}_5$ with $i\equiv 2\pmod 5$.

hard.q: Hensel's lemma can fail when $f'(x_0)\equiv 0\pmod p$. Which example correctly exhibits this failure?
choices: $f(x)=x^2-p$ over $\mathbb{Z}_p$, with $x_0=0$: $f(0)\equiv 0\pmod p$ and $f'(0)=0\pmod p$; indeed $\sqrt{p}\notin\mathbb{Z}_p$ since $v_p(p)=1$ is odd
$f(x)=x^2+1$ over $\mathbb{Z}_5$, with $x_0=2$: $f'(2)=4\equiv 0\pmod 5$, so Hensel fails and $\sqrt{-1}\notin\mathbb{Q}_5$
$f(x)=x^3-x$ over $\mathbb{Z}_3$, with $x_0=1$: $f'(1)=2\not\equiv 0\pmod 3$, illustrating the failure condition
Hensel's lemma never fails as long as $f\in\mathbb{Z}_p[x]$
hard.explain: For $f(x)=x^2-p$ the seed $x_0=0$ satisfies $f(0)=-p\equiv 0\pmod p$ but $f'(0)=0\pmod p$. The Newton step would require dividing by $0$; and indeed $\sqrt{p}\notin\mathbb{Z}_p$ (nor $\mathbb{Q}_p$) since its $p$-adic valuation would be $1/2$, which is not an integer.

hard.q: Apply one Hensel step to $f(x)=x^2-2$ over $\mathbb{Z}_{17}$ starting from $x_0=6$ (since $6^2=36=2+2\cdot 17\equiv 2\pmod{17}$). Compute $x_1\bmod{17^2}=x_0 - f(x_0)/f'(x_0)\bmod{289}$.
hard.explain: Starting from $x_0=6$ with $6^2\equiv 2\pmod{17}$: $f(6)=34=2\cdot 17$. Digit $a_1 = -(f(x_0)/p)\cdot(f'(x_0))^{-1}\bmod p = -2\cdot(12^{-1}\bmod 17)\bmod 17$. Since $12\cdot 10=120\equiv 1\pmod{17}$, $a_1=-2\cdot 10\bmod 17=(-20)\bmod 17=14$. So $x_1=6+14\cdot 17=244$. Verify: $244^2=59536$ and $59536-2=59534=206\cdot 289$, confirming $244^2\equiv 2\pmod{289}$.

hard.q: For $p$ an odd prime, $\sqrt{p-1}$ exists in $\mathbb{Z}_p$ iff $p-1$ is a quadratic residue mod $p$. Since $p-1\equiv -1\pmod p$, which primes $p$ allow $\sqrt{-1}\in\mathbb{Z}_p$?
choices: All odd primes $p$
Primes $p\equiv 1\pmod 4$, because $-1$ is a QR mod $p$ iff $p\equiv 1\pmod 4$ by Euler's criterion
Primes $p\equiv 3\pmod 4$ only
No odd prime $p$, since $-1<0$ in $\mathbb{R}$
hard.explain: By Euler's criterion, $(-1)^{(p-1)/2}\equiv 1\pmod p$ iff $(p-1)/2$ is even, i.e. $4\mid p-1$, i.e. $p\equiv 1\pmod 4$. For such primes Hensel's lemma lifts a mod-$p$ square root of $-1$ to a genuine element of $\mathbb{Z}_p$. For $p\equiv 3\pmod 4$, $-1$ is a QNR and no lift exists.

questions.q: The Hasse-Minkowski theorem says that a quadratic form $Q$ over $\mathbb{Q}$ represents zero nontrivially over $\mathbb{Q}$ iff:
choices: it does so over $\mathbb{R}$
it does so over some $\mathbb{Q}_p$
it does so over $\mathbb{R}$ and over every $\mathbb{Q}_p$
its discriminant is a perfect square
questions.explain: Hasse-Minkowski: for quadratic forms, local solvability at all places (the real place plus every $p$-adic place) is equivalent to global solvability over $\mathbb{Q}$.

questions.q: Consider $x^2 + y^2 = 3$ over $\mathbb{Q}$. Using Hasse-Minkowski, which obstruction rules out rational solutions?
choices: Real obstruction: the left side is non-negative so cannot equal $3$
Local obstruction at $p=3$: the equation has no primitive solutions mod $9$
There is no obstruction; rational solutions exist
Local obstruction at $p=2$: $x,y$ must both be even, forcing a contradiction
questions.explain: $x^2+y^2=3$ is clearly solvable in $\mathbb{R}$. The $3$-adic obstruction: mod $3$, squares are $\{0,1\}$, so $x^2+y^2\in\{0,1,2\}\bmod 3$, never $3\equiv 0$ with $(x,y)$ primitive. This local failure forces no rational solution.

questions.q: The local-global principle FAILS for some cubic forms. What kind of object typically measures this failure for elliptic curves over $\mathbb{Q}$?
choices: The discriminant
The conductor
The Tate-Shafarevich group $\Sha(E/\mathbb{Q})$
The j-invariant
questions.explain: $\Sha(E/\mathbb{Q})$ parametrises locally trivial principal homogeneous spaces for $E$ — objects that have points in every $\mathbb{Q}_p$ and in $\mathbb{R}$ but not in $\mathbb{Q}$. It is the standard measure of local-global failure for elliptic curves.

hard.q: The Hasse-Minkowski theorem applies to quadratic forms but NOT to cubic forms in general. Which of the following is the standard counterexample to the local-global principle for cubics?
choices: $3x^3+4y^3+5z^3=0$: Selmer showed it has points over $\mathbb{R}$ and all $\mathbb{Q}_p$ but no rational points
$x^3+y^3+z^3=0$: has only the trivial solution over $\mathbb{Q}$ but nontrivial $p$-adic solutions
$x^2+y^2+z^2=0$: a quadratic form where local-global fails at $p=2$
$x^3-2=0$: has a real root but no $p$-adic root for any prime $p$
hard.explain: Selmer's cubic $3x^3+4y^3+5z^3=0$ is the classical counterexample: it is solvable nontrivially in $\mathbb{R}$ and in every $\mathbb{Q}_p$, yet has no nontrivial solution in $\mathbb{Q}$. This demonstrates that the Hasse principle can fail for degree-$3$ forms, unlike degree-$2$ forms covered by Hasse-Minkowski.

hard.q: To verify local solvability of $x^2+y^2+z^2=0$ over all $\mathbb{Q}_p$, one must check each prime. At which prime does the only obstruction occur, and why does it vanish?
choices: At $p=2$: a mod-$8$ argument shows $x^2+y^2+z^2\equiv 0$ has nontrivial solutions mod $8$, and Hensel lifts them
At $p=3$: the form is isotropic mod $3$ by a counting argument (there are $3^2$ pairs $(x,y)$ mod $3$, and at least one gives $-x^2-y^2$ a quadratic residue)
At the real place: $x^2+y^2+z^2=0$ has no real solution (other than $0$), so there IS a real obstruction and Hasse-Minkowski correctly predicts no rational solution
At no prime; the form is everywhere locally solvable but fails globally due to the Brauer group
hard.explain: The form $x^2+y^2+z^2$ is positive-definite over $\mathbb{R}$, so $x^2+y^2+z^2=0$ has only the trivial real solution. This real (Archimedean) obstruction is exactly the obstruction predicted by Hasse-Minkowski, which requires isotropy at ALL places including $\mathbb{R}$.

hard.q: Adèles $\mathbb{A}_\mathbb{Q}$ package all completions of $\mathbb{Q}$ simultaneously. Which of the following correctly states the adèlic reformulation of the Hasse principle for a variety $V/\mathbb{Q}$?
choices: $V(\mathbb{Q})\ne\emptyset$ iff $V(\mathbb{A}_\mathbb{Q})\ne\emptyset$; when this equivalence holds we say $V$ satisfies the Hasse principle
$V(\mathbb{Q})\ne\emptyset$ iff $V(\mathbb{Q}_p)\ne\emptyset$ for the prime $p=\operatorname{char}(V)$
The Hasse principle holds for $V$ iff $V(\mathbb{A}_\mathbb{Q})$ is compact
$V(\mathbb{A}_\mathbb{Q})=V(\mathbb{Q})$ as sets, since adèles contain $\mathbb{Q}$ diagonally
hard.explain:
The adèlic Hasse principle: $V$ satisfies the Hasse principle if $V(\mathbb{A}_\mathbb{Q})
e\emptyset\Rightarrow V(\mathbb{Q})
e\emptyset$. Having adèlic points means having points over every completion $\mathbb{Q}_v$ (all $\mathbb{Q}_p$ and $\mathbb{R}$). Failure of the Hasse principle means $V(\mathbb{A}_\mathbb{Q})
e\emptyset$ yet $V(\mathbb{Q})=\emptyset$; the Brauer-Manin obstruction is one systematic source of such failures.
